title: Boece
summary: The repository XML is a local copy or version of U-M CME item ChaucerBo, keyed from Richard Morris's 1868 EETS edition of Geoffrey Chaucer's Middle English prose translation of Boethius's Consolation of Philosophy. Morris edited British Library Add MS 10340 as his base and collated Cambridge University Library MS Ii.3.21; bracketed whole words generally come from the Cambridge witness. The XML also includes Morris's introduction and appendices but omits the printed synoptic marginal gloss, most textual footnotes, and glossarial index.
review_notes: Boethius is author of the Latin source work; Chaucer is translator and adapter of the Middle English Boece; Morris is editor of the immediate edition.
The edition has a base-witness-plus-collation lineage, not a single-witness lineage.
The local 1999 availability notice and U-M's current No Copyright–United States statement are preserved as separate provider statements.

date_statements.label: production
date_statements.value: first quarter of the fifteenth century

notes: Boece occupies fols. 3r–40r in two columns.

entities.label: Cambridge University Library MS Ii.3.21
entities.description: The Cambridge manuscript against which Morris collated his Add MS 10340 base; the reviewed U-M XML and Morris edition identify it as a witness of Chaucer's Boece.
notes: No item-level Cambridge catalog record supporting a precise production date, foliation, or fuller contents was verified in this review.

entities.label: Boethius, De consolatione philosophiae
entities.description: The late-antique Latin prosimetrum translated and adapted by Chaucer; Chaucer also drew on Jean de Meun's French translation and Latin commentary traditions.

scope.notes: Synoptic marginal gloss, most textual footnotes, and glossarial index are omitted.
Add MS 10340 is the base manuscript.

scope.notes: Whole words in brackets generally come from the Cambridge manuscript.

subject.description: Chaucer's five-book Middle English prose translation

scope.notes: This work-level relation is not a claim that Morris used a particular surviving Latin manuscript.

rights.component: Boethius's ancient source and Chaucer's fourteenth-century Middle English translation
rights.basis: Both underlying works predate modern copyright by centuries.
First published in 1868; Morris died in 1894.

rights.component: Materials presented in U-M CME item ChaucerBo
rights.basis: U-M expressly labels the item public domain and No Copyright–United States.
notes: The 1999 XML availability statement permits personal, research, and teaching copies but forbids redistribution by content providers; whether the current statement supersedes it is unresolved.

rights.basis: Underlying texts and edition are public domain in the United States, but the XML embeds an express redistribution restriction.

rights.component: Any modern manuscript photographs or scans
rights.jurisdiction: Provider terms and intended place of reuse
rights.basis: The manuscript text is old, but no reusable image set or image-specific license was verified.

editorial_practices.description: Morris uses Add MS 10340 as base and collates CUL Ii.3.21; bracketed whole words generally derive from Cambridge.
The encoding omits the synoptic marginal gloss, most textual footnotes, and glossarial index while retaining most other printed material.

evidence.citation: General Corpus, CME/source/CME_phase_1-2/ChaucerBo.xml, HEADER and editorial declaration.
evidence.summary: Identifies the 1999 U-M encoding, Morris 1868 source, base and collation witnesses, omissions, bracket conventions, and provider terms.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, CME item ChaucerBo.
evidence.summary: Confirms the Morris edition, public full text, contents, and NoC-US statement.

evidence.citation: Richard Morris, ed., Chaucer's Translation of Boethius's De Consolatione Philosophiae, EETS ES 5 (1868), Project Gutenberg 42083 and Internet Archive.
evidence.summary: Public reproduction confirming title, edition statement, source manuscripts, contents, and editorial practices.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Add MS 10340.
evidence.summary: Dates and describes the parchment manuscript, locates Boece at fols. 3r–40r, identifies Morris's edition, and reports digital images currently unavailable.

evidence.citation: Cambridge University Library, Manuscripts and University Archives department information.
evidence.summary: Provides the official institutional route for enquiries about Cambridge University Library manuscript shelfmarks; the witness identification and collation claims come from the repository XML and Morris edition, not from an item-level online catalog record.

evidence.citation: University of Georgia Press, Sources of the Boece, ed. Tim William Machan, with Alastair J. Minnis (2005).
evidence.summary: Dates Chaucer's translation around 1380 and documents use of the Latin Vulgate Consolatio, Jean de Meun's French translation, and Nicholas Trevet's commentary.

evidence.citation: U.S. Copyright Office, Circular 15A, Duration of Copyright.
evidence.summary: Supports the U.S. public-domain term assessment for an 1868 publication.

open_questions.question: Does U-M's current NoC-US statement supersede the 1999 XML restriction against redistribution by commercial or not-for-profit content providers?
next_steps: Obtain a written interpretation from U-M before redistributing the XML or a close derivative commercially.
Keep copyright status and provider-contract status separate.

open_questions.question: Can Chaucer's translation date be narrowed beyond ‘around 1380’?
next_steps: Consult Machan and Minnis on the occasion of Chaucer's Boece and current chronology scholarship.

open_questions.question: Which individual words and passages in the electronic text derive from CUL Ii.3.21 rather than Add MS 10340?
next_steps: Compare Morris's full footnotes and synoptic gloss with both witnesses; bracketed whole words are only a first indicator.
